---
title: 'Solutions with Notes for Walkthroughs and Exercises for *Machine Learning for Data Analytics with Python*'  
author: "Dr. Chester Ismay"  
format:
  html:
    theme: flatly
    toc: true
    toc-floating: true 
engine: knitr  
execute:
  warning: false
---

```{python}
import pandas as pd

# Display all columns
pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)

# Display all outputs from each cell
from IPython.core.interactiveshell import InteractiveShell
InteractiveShell.ast_node_interactivity = "all"
```

# Intro: Getting Started with Machine Learning for Data-Driven Decisions

## Walkthrough #1: Setting Up the Python Environment for ML

If you haven't already installed Python, Jupyter, and the necessary packages, there are instructions on the course repo in the README to do so [here](https://github.com/ismayc/oreilly-data-analysis-with-python/blob/main/README.md). 

You can also install the packages directly in a Jupyter notebook with

```{python}
#| eval: false
!pip install pandas seaborn matplotlib scikit-learn mlxtend
```

If you aren't able to do this on your machine, you may want to check out [Google Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/). It's a free service that allows you to run Jupyter notebooks in the cloud. Alternatively, I've set up some temporary notebooks on Binder [here](https://mybinder.org/v2/gh/ismayc/oreilly-ml-for-data-analytics-with-python/HEAD?urlpath=%2Fdoc%2Ftree%2Fexercises.ipynb) that you can work with online as well.

Run the following code to check that each of the needed packages are installed. If you get an error, you may need to install the package(s) again.


```{python}
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error, r2_score
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score, confusion_matrix
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score
from mlxtend.frequent_patterns import apriori, association_rules
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, cross_validate
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV
```

```{python}
# Load dataset
telco_churn_raw = pd.read_csv('telco-customer-churn.csv')
```


## Exercise #1: Setting Up the Python Environment

By completing this exercise, you will be able to  

- Import necessary Python packages  
- Check for successful package loading  
- Load datasets into Python

Follow the instructions above in Walkthrough #1 to check for correct installation 
of necessary packages.

```{python}
# Load dataset
marketing_campaign_raw = pd.read_csv('marketing_campaign.csv')
```

---

# Module 1: Data Understanding and Preprocessing for Machine Learning

## Walkthrough #2: Exploring and Preprocessing Data with Pandas & Seaborn

### Inspect a dataset using Pandas

> 🕵️‍♂️ *"Let's start by getting a feel for our data. `.info()` gives us the full structure: how many rows, columns, and the types of each one."*

```{python}
telco_churn_raw
telco_churn_raw.info()
```

> 🔎 *"Now let's do a quality control check. Missing values are like potholes in our data road."*

```{python}
telco_churn_raw.isnull().sum()
telco_churn_raw.duplicated().sum()
```

### Handle missing values and clean data

> 🧼 *"Always make a copy before modifying. Think of it like 'Save As' for data."*

```{python}
telco_churn = telco_churn_raw.copy()
```

> 💡 *"Let's patch those potholes using the median. It's less sensitive to extreme outliers than the mean."*

```{python}
telco_churn['MonthlyCharges'] = telco_churn['MonthlyCharges'].\
    fillna(telco_churn['MonthlyCharges'].median())
telco_churn['AvgServiceUsageScore'] = telco_churn['AvgServiceUsageScore'].\
    fillna(telco_churn['AvgServiceUsageScore'].median())
telco_churn.info()
```

```{python}
telco_churn['SeniorCitizen'] = telco_churn['SeniorCitizen'].astype('category')
telco_churn['Churn'] = telco_churn['Churn'].map({'Yes': 1, 'No': 0})
```

```{python}
telco_churn.describe(include='all')
```

### Create visualizations to identify key business trends

```{python}
plt.clf()
sns.violinplot(data=telco_churn, x='Churn', y='tenure', inner='quartile')
plt.title('Violin Plot of Tenure by Churn')
plt.show();
```

```{python}
plt.clf()
sns.histplot(data=telco_churn, x='AvgServiceUsageScore')
plt.title('Histogram of Average Service Usage Score')
plt.show();
```

```{python}
plt.clf()
sns.histplot(data=telco_churn, x='MonthlyCharges')
plt.title('Histogram of Monthly Charges')
plt.show();
```

```{python}
plt.clf()
sns.scatterplot(data=telco_churn, x='AvgServiceUsageScore', y='MonthlyCharges')
plt.title('Average Service Usage Score vs. Monthly Charges')
plt.show();
```

```{python}
plt.clf()
sns.countplot(data=telco_churn, x='InternetService', hue='Churn')
plt.title('Churn Rate by Internet Service Type')
plt.show();
```

---

## Exercise #2: Exploring and Preprocessing Data with Pandas & Seaborn

### Inspect a dataset using Pandas

```{python}
marketing_campaign_raw
marketing_campaign_raw.info()
```

```{python}
marketing_campaign_raw.isnull().sum()
marketing_campaign_raw.duplicated().sum()
```

### Handle missing values and clean data

```{python}
marketing_campaign = marketing_campaign_raw.copy()
marketing_campaign['Income'] = marketing_campaign['Income'].fillna(marketing_campaign['Income'].median())
```

```{python}
marketing_campaign["TotalChildren"] = marketing_campaign["Kidhome"] + marketing_campaign["Teenhome"]
marketing_campaign["TotalSpent"] = (
    marketing_campaign["MntWines"] + marketing_campaign["MntFruits"] +
    marketing_campaign["MntMeatProducts"] + marketing_campaign["MntFishProducts"] +
    marketing_campaign["MntSweetProducts"] + marketing_campaign["MntGoldProds"]
)
```

```{python}
marketing_campaign["Dt_Customer"] = pd.to_datetime(marketing_campaign["Dt_Customer"], errors='coerce')
marketing_campaign.info()
```

```{python}
marketing_campaign.describe(include='all')
```

### Create visualizations to identify key business trends

```{python}
plt.clf()
sns.violinplot(data=marketing_campaign, x='Response', y='Income', inner='quartile')
plt.title('Violin Plot of Income by Campaign Response')
plt.show();
```

```{python}
plt.clf()
sns.histplot(data=marketing_campaign, x='TotalSpent')
plt.title('Histogram of Total Spent')
plt.show();
```

```{python}
plt.clf()
sns.scatterplot(data=marketing_campaign, x='Income', y='TotalSpent', hue='Response', alpha=0.7)
plt.title('Income vs. Total Spent by Campaign Response')
plt.show();
```

```{python}
plt.clf()
sns.countplot(data=marketing_campaign, x='Education', hue='Response')
plt.title('Campaign Response by Education Level')
plt.show();
```

### Interpretation Questions

1. Looking at the violin plot of Income by Response, which group (responders or non-responders) shows more variability in income?
2. Based on your scatter plot, do higher-income customers tend to spend more? Is this relationship strong or weak?
3. Which education level shows the highest response rate? What marketing implications might this have?

> 💡 **Sample Answers:**
>
> 1. Non-responders have a long tail on the high end, indicating more variability in income compared to responders.
>
> 2. Not necessarily. There is a positive trend for both responders and non-responders but it is hard to detect trends because of the outliers in income.
>
> 3. Estimated Response Rates:

> PhD: ~100 responders out of ~485 total ≈ 21%
> Graduation: ~150 responders out of ~1,125 total ≈ 13%
> Master: ~55 responders out of ~370 total ≈ 15%
> 2n Cycle: ~25 responders out of ~205 total ≈ 12%
> Basic: Nearly 0 responders out of ~55 total ≈ ~0%

> PhD holders show the highest response rate at roughly 21%. PhD holders are the most receptive audience. If marketing budgets are limited, focusing resources on this segment would likely yield the best return on investment. The near-zero response rate for Basic suggests either a mismatch between the offering and this demographic, or possibly that the sample size is too small to draw conclusions. Before abandoning this segment entirely, it would be worth investigating why.

---

# Module 2: Supervised Learning for Business Decisions

## Walkthrough #3: Build a Regression Model for Pricing Optimization

### Split the data into training and validation sets

```{python}
X = telco_churn[['AvgServiceUsageScore']]
y = telco_churn['MonthlyCharges']

scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(X_scaled, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=2025)
```

### Train a linear regression model

```{python}
lr = LinearRegression()
lr.fit(X_train, y_train)

print(f"Intercept: {lr.intercept_:.2f}")
print(f"Coefficient (usage_scaled → price): {lr.coef_[0]:.2f}")
```

### Evaluate model performance on the validation set

```{python}
y_pred = lr.predict(X_val)

r2 = r2_score(y_val, y_pred)
mae = mean_absolute_error(y_val, y_pred)

print(f"R-squared: {r2:.2f}")
print(f"Mean Absolute Error: {mae:.2f}")
```

---

## Exercise #3: Build a Regression Model for Pricing Optimization

### Split the data into training and validation sets

```{python}
X_m = marketing_campaign[['Income']]
y_m = marketing_campaign['TotalSpent']

y_m = y_m.reset_index(drop=True)

scaler_m = StandardScaler()
X_m_scaled = scaler_m.fit_transform(X_m)

X_m_train, X_m_val, y_m_train, y_m_val = train_test_split(X_m_scaled, y_m, test_size=0.2, random_state=2025)
```

### Train a linear regression model

```{python}
lr_m = LinearRegression()
lr_m.fit(X_m_train, y_m_train)

print(f"Intercept: {lr_m.intercept_:.2f}")
print(f"Coefficient (Income_scaled → TotalSpent): {lr_m.coef_[0]:.2f}")
```

### Evaluate model performance on the validation set

```{python}
y_m_pred = lr_m.predict(X_m_val)

r2_m = r2_score(y_m_val, y_m_pred)
mae_m = mean_absolute_error(y_m_val, y_m_pred)

print(f"R-squared: {r2_m:.2f}")
print(f"Mean Absolute Error: {mae_m:.2f}")
```

### Interpretation Questions

1. Is your R-squared higher or lower than the telco churn model? What might explain the difference?
2. If MAE is $200, what does that mean in practical terms for predicting customer spending?
3. Would you trust this model for making budget decisions? Why or why not?

> 💡 **Sample Answers:**
>
> 1. The Exercise #3 regression model (marketing campaign) has an R² of 0.48, while the Walkthrough #3 telco churn model has an R² of 0.80. So the marketing model's R-squared is substantially lower.
Possible explanations for this difference:

> Weaker linear relationship: Income and TotalSpent in the marketing data may not have as strong or as linear a relationship as AvgServiceUsageScore and MonthlyCharges in the telco data.
>
> Different domains: Telecom service usage is more directly tied to charges (usage-based pricing), whereas retail spending behavior is more discretionary and varied.

> 2. On average, the model's predictions of customer spending (TotalSpent) are off by about $200 in either direction. Given that the mean TotalSpent is approximately $606 and the median is $396, an error of $200 represents a significant portion of a typical customer's spending. For practical purposes, predictions could easily be 30-50% off for many customers, making precise individual-level forecasting challenging.
>
> 3. I would be cautious about using this model for budget decisions. Here's why:

> R² of 0.48 means the model only explains about 48% of the variance in customer spending. More than half of the variation remains unexplained.
> MAE of ~$324 is quite large relative to typical spending amounts, suggesting predictions could be significantly off for individual customers.
> Single predictor limitation: Using only Income ignores other important factors like purchase history, demographics, or engagement metrics that likely influence spending.

> However, the model might still be useful for:

> Rough directional guidance or segmentation (higher income generally means higher spending)
> Identifying broad trends rather than precise individual predictions
> A baseline to improve upon by adding more features

> For high-stakes budget decisions, I would recommend incorporating additional predictors, testing more sophisticated models, and validating against held-out data before relying on these predictions.

---

## Walkthrough #4: Implement a Classification Model for Customer Churn

### Split the data into training and validation sets

```{python}
features = ['tenure', 'SeniorCitizen', 'ServiceCount', 'InternetScore', 'AvgServiceUsageScore']
X = telco_churn[features]
y = telco_churn['Churn']

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=2025)
```

### Train a Random Forest classification model

```{python}
clf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=2025)
clf.fit(X_train, y_train)
```

### Evaluate model performance on the validation set

```{python}
y_pred = clf.predict(X_test)

print(f"Accuracy: {accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred):.2f}")
print(f"Precision: {precision_score(y_test, y_pred):.2f}")
print(f"Recall: {recall_score(y_test, y_pred):.2f}")

cm = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred)
labels = ['No Churn', 'Churn']
cm_telco_churn = pd.DataFrame(cm, index=labels, columns=labels)

print("\nConfusion Matrix (formatted):")
print(cm_telco_churn)
```

### Quick Reference: When to Use Which Metric

| Situation | Metric | Why |
|-----------|--------|-----|
| Predicting continuous values | R², MAE | Measures prediction error |
| Balanced classes | Accuracy | Overall correctness |
| Cost of false positives is high | Precision | Minimize wrong positive predictions |
| Cost of false negatives is high | Recall | Catch all actual positives |
| Need balance | F1-Score | Harmonic mean of precision/recall |


---

## Exercise #4: Implement a Classification Model for Customer Churn

### Split the data into training and validation sets

```{python}
features_m = ['Income', 'TotalSpent', 'TotalChildren']
X_m_class = marketing_campaign[features_m]
y_m_class = marketing_campaign['Response']

X_m_train_class, X_m_val_class, y_m_train_class, y_m_val_class = train_test_split(
    X_m_class, y_m_class, test_size=0.2, random_state=2025
)
```

### Train a Random Forest classification model

```{python}
clf_m = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=2025)
clf_m.fit(X_m_train_class, y_m_train_class)
```

### Evaluate model performance on the validation set

```{python}
y_m_pred_class = clf_m.predict(X_m_val_class)

print(f"Accuracy: {accuracy_score(y_m_val_class, y_m_pred_class):.2f}")
print(f"Precision: {precision_score(y_m_val_class, y_m_pred_class):.2f}")
print(f"Recall: {recall_score(y_m_val_class, y_m_pred_class):.2f}")

labels = ['No Response', 'Response']
cm_marketing_response = pd.DataFrame(confusion_matrix(y_m_val_class, y_m_pred_class), index=labels, columns=labels)

print("\nConfusion Matrix (formatted):")
print(cm_marketing_response)
```

### Interpretation Questions

1. Compare your precision and recall. Which is higher, and what does that imply about the model's tendencies?
2. Looking at your confusion matrix, is the model better at identifying responders or non-responders?
3. If running a marketing campaign costs $50 per contact, which metric matters more: precision or recall?

> 💡 **Sample Answers:**
>
> 1. Precision is slightly higher than recall, though both are quite low.
What this implies:

> Low recall (20%) means the model is missing 80% of actual responders. It's very conservative about predicting someone will respond.
> Low precision (27%) means that when the model does predict a response, it's only correct about 27% of the time.
> The model has a strong tendency to predict "No Response" for most customers. It's essentially playing it safe by defaulting to the majority class, which results in missing most of the actual responders.
>
> 2. The model is much better at identifying non-responders.
Breaking this down:

> Non-responders correctly identified: 364 out of 394 (92%)
> Responders correctly identified: 11 out of 54 (20%)

> This is a classic pattern with imbalanced datasets. The marketing campaign data has far more non-responders than responders (about 85% vs 15%), so the model learns to predict the majority class most of the time. It achieves high overall accuracy (84%) by being good at the easy task (identifying non-responders) while largely failing at the harder, more valuable task (identifying responders).
>
> 3. It depends on the value of a successful conversion, but generally precision matters more in this scenario.
> Here's the reasoning:
> If precision matters more (cost-focused scenario):

> Each contact costs $50, so you want to minimize wasted spending on people who won't respond.
> Higher precision means a larger proportion of the people you contact will actually respond.
With current precision of 27%, you're spending $50 on roughly 4 people for every 1 actual responder. That's about $200 cost per successful contact.
Improving precision reduces your cost per acquisition.

> When recall would matter more:

> If the lifetime value of a customer is extremely high (say, $5,000+), missing potential responders becomes very costly.
If you have a limited-time offer or a one-shot opportunity where you can't reach out again.
If market share or growth is more important than campaign efficiency.

> Practical recommendation:
Calculate the break-even point. If a successful response generates more than $185 in value (since $50 / 0.27 precision ≈ $185 cost per true positive), the campaign could still be profitable despite the low precision. But improving precision would make the campaign more efficient and reduce the risk of overspending on unlikely responders.

---

# Module 3: Unsupervised Learning and Pattern Discovery in Business

## Walkthrough #5: Exploring K-Means Clustering for Customer Segmentation

### Apply K-Means clustering to segment customers

```{python}
telco_churn['ContractType'] = telco_churn['Contract'].map({'Month-to-month': 0, 'One year': 1, 'Two year': 2})
features = ['tenure', 'ServiceCount', 'AvgServiceUsageScore', 'MonthlyCharges', 'InternetScore', 'ContractType']
X = telco_churn[features]

scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)
```

### Determine the optimal number of clusters using the Elbow Method

```{python}
inertia = []
k_range = range(1, 11)

for k in k_range:
    kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=k, random_state=2025)
    kmeans.fit(X_scaled)
    inertia.append(kmeans.inertia_)

plt.clf()
plt.plot(k_range, inertia, marker='o')
plt.xlabel('Number of clusters (k)')
plt.ylabel('Inertia')
plt.title('Elbow Method for Optimal k')
plt.show()
```

### Verify using the silhouette score

```{python}
silhouette_scores = {}

for k in range(3, 7):
    kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=k, random_state=2025)
    labels = kmeans.fit_predict(X_scaled)
    score = silhouette_score(X_scaled, labels)
    silhouette_scores[k] = score

silhouette_scores
```

### Fit K-means and assign cluster labels

```{python}
optimal_k = 3
kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=optimal_k, random_state=2025)
telco_churn['Cluster'] = kmeans.fit_predict(X_scaled)
```

### Visualize customer segments

```{python}
plt.clf()
sns.scatterplot(data=telco_churn, x='tenure', y='AvgServiceUsageScore', hue='Cluster', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Customer Segments via K-Means')
plt.show();
```

---

## Exercise #5: Exploring K-Means Clustering for Customer Segmentation

### Apply K-Means clustering to segment customers

```{python}
features_cluster = ['Income', 'TotalSpent', 'TotalChildren']
X_cluster = marketing_campaign[features_cluster]

scaler = StandardScaler()
X_cluster_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_cluster)
```

### Determine the optimal number of clusters

```{python}
inertia = []
k_range = range(1, 11)

for k in k_range:
    kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=k, random_state=2025)
    kmeans.fit(X_cluster_scaled)
    inertia.append(kmeans.inertia_)

plt.clf()
plt.plot(k_range, inertia, marker='o')
plt.xlabel('Number of clusters (k)')
plt.ylabel('Inertia')
plt.title('Elbow Method for Optimal k')
plt.show();
```

### Verify using the silhouette score

```{python}
silhouette_scores = {}

for k in range(3, 7):
    kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=k, random_state=2025)
    labels = kmeans.fit_predict(X_cluster_scaled)
    score = silhouette_score(X_cluster_scaled, labels)
    silhouette_scores[k] = score

silhouette_scores
```

### Fit K-means and assign cluster labels

```{python}
optimal_k = 5
kmeans_final = KMeans(n_clusters=optimal_k, random_state=2025)
marketing_campaign['Cluster'] = kmeans_final.fit_predict(X_cluster_scaled)
```

### Visualize customer segments

```{python}
import matplotlib.ticker as ticker
plt.clf()
sns.scatterplot(data=marketing_campaign, x='TotalChildren', y='TotalSpent', hue='Cluster', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Customer Segments via K-Means Clustering')
plt.gca().xaxis.set_major_locator(ticker.MultipleLocator(1))
plt.show();
```

### Interpretation Questions

1. How did you decide on the optimal k? Did the elbow method and silhouette scores agree?
2. Looking at your 2D visualization, do the clusters seem well-separated or do they overlap?

> 💡 **Sample Answers:**
>
> 1. The silhouette scores for k=3 through k=6 were:

> k=3: 0.435
> k=4: 0.438
> k=5: 0.443
> k=6: 0.429

> The elbow plot shows a gradual bend around k=4 or k=5, without a sharp "elbow." The silhouette scores peak at k=5 (0.443), which is what was chosen.
> They roughly agree, though neither gives a definitive answer. The elbow is subtle, and silhouette scores are very close for k=3 through k=5. The choice of k=5 is reasonable but somewhat subjective.

> 2. The boundaries between clusters aren't crisp, suggesting customers near cluster edges share characteristics with neighboring segments
>
> 3. You might see: high-income/high-spend families, budget-conscious parents, childless high-spenders, low-engagement customers, etc.

---

## Walkthrough #6: Market Basket Analysis with Apriori Algorithm

### Prepare transactional data

```{python}
service_cols = ['OnlineSecurity', 'OnlineBackup', 'DeviceProtection', 'TechSupport', 'StreamingTV', 'StreamingMovies', 'PhoneService']
telco_churn_basket = telco_churn[service_cols].astype(bool)
telco_churn_basket
```

### Apply the Apriori algorithm

```{python}
frequent_itemsets = apriori(telco_churn_basket, min_support=0.2, use_colnames=True)
frequent_itemsets
```

### Generate association rules

```{python}
rules = association_rules(frequent_itemsets, metric="confidence", min_threshold=0.6)
rules = rules[['antecedents', 'consequents', 'support', 'confidence', 'lift']]
rules
```

---

## Exercise #6: Market Basket Analysis with Apriori Algorithm

### Prepare transactional data

```{python}
product_cols = ['MntWines', 'MntFruits', 'MntMeatProducts', 'MntFishProducts', 'MntSweetProducts', 'MntGoldProds']
basket = marketing_campaign[product_cols].applymap(lambda x: 1 if x > 0 else 0)
basket.columns = ['Wines', 'Fruits', 'Meat', 'Fish', 'Sweets', 'Gold']
basket = basket.astype(bool)
basket.head()
```

### Apply the Apriori algorithm

```{python}
frequent_itemsets = apriori(basket, min_support=0.2, use_colnames=True)
frequent_itemsets.sort_values(by='support', ascending=False)
```

### Generate association rules

```{python}
rules = association_rules(frequent_itemsets, metric="confidence", min_threshold=0.6)
rules = rules[['antecedents', 'consequents', 'support', 'confidence', 'lift']]
rules = rules.sort_values(by='lift', ascending=False)
rules.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)
rules
```

### Interpretation Questions

1. Which product category appears in the most frequent itemsets?
2. Find a rule with high confidence but low lift. Why might this rule be less useful?
3. Identify one actionable insight: what product bundle would you recommend?

> 💡 **Sample Answers:**
>
> 1. Meat and Wines appear most frequently. These are core products that almost everyone buys.
>
> 2. Rules with lift ~1 are no better than random chance, even if confidence is high. The consequent is just commonly purchased anyway.
>
> 3. Bundle Fruits + Fish together (they have lift > 1), or create a premium box with Sweets, Gold, and Fish for customers who already buy Wines and Meat.

# Module 4: Implementing and Evaluating ML Models

## Walkthrough #7: Exploring Cross-Validation for Model Evaluation

### Split data into training and validation sets

> 🧪 *"We're back to modeling churn, but this time with an extra layer of rigor. First, we prep our feature set for a logistic regression model, which means scaling is a must."*

```{python}
X = telco_churn[['AvgServiceUsageScore', 'tenure', 'MonthlyCharges']]
y = telco_churn['Churn']

scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X_scaled, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=2025)
```

### Train a classification model using logistic regression

```{python}
logreg = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, solver='liblinear')
```

### Apply k-fold cross-validation

> 🔁 *"Here's the magic: cross-validation. Instead of one train/test split, we split the training set into 5 folds and rotate through them. This helps us get a much more stable estimate of how the model performs."*

```{python}
cv_results = cross_validate(
    logreg, 
    X_train, 
    y_train, 
    cv=5, 
    scoring=['accuracy', 'precision', 'recall', 'f1'],
    return_train_score=True
)
```

### Compare metrics across folds

```{python}
def show_metric(name, results):
    scores = results[f"test_{name}"]
    print(f"{name.capitalize()}: {scores.mean():.3f} ± {scores.std():.3f}")

for metric in ['accuracy', 'precision', 'recall', 'f1']:
    show_metric(metric, cv_results)
```

**Interpretation**

- Accuracy → Overall correctness of predictions
- Precision → How many predicted churns were actual churns
- Recall → How many churns were correctly identified
- F1-Score → Balances precision & recall

Cross-validation ensures that your model generalizes better to unseen data by reducing the risk of overfitting on a single split.

---

## Exercise #7: Exploring Cross-Validation for Model Evaluation

### Split data into training and validation sets

```{python}
X_m = marketing_campaign[['Income', 'TotalSpent', 'TotalChildren']]
y_m = marketing_campaign['Response']

scaler = StandardScaler()
X_m_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_m)

X_m_train, X_m_test, y_m_train, y_m_test = train_test_split(X_m_scaled, y_m, test_size=0.2, random_state=2025)
```

### Train a classification model using logistic regression

```{python}
logreg_m = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, solver='liblinear')
```

### Apply k-fold cross-validation

```{python}
cv_results_m = cross_validate(
    logreg_m, 
    X_m_train, 
    y_m_train, 
    cv=5, 
    scoring=['accuracy', 'precision', 'recall', 'f1'],
    return_train_score=True
)
```

### Compare metrics across folds

```{python}
for m in ["accuracy", "precision", "recall", "f1"]:
    show_metric(m, cv_results_m)
```

**Interpretation**

- Accuracy → Overall correctness of predictions
- Precision → How many predicted responders were actual responders
- Recall → How many actual responders were correctly identified
- F1-Score → Harmonic mean of precision and recall

<!--
### Interpretation Questions

1. How consistent are your metrics across folds? (Look at the ± values)
2. Which metric shows the most variability? What might cause this?
3. Compare your cross-validation results to the single train-test split in Exercise #4. Are they similar?

> 💡 **Sample Answers:**
>
> 1. Accuracy is very consistent (small ±). Recall and F1 show more variability due to class imbalance.
>
> 2. Recall typically shows the most variability because it depends heavily on correctly identifying the minority class (responders), which varies across folds.
>
> 3. Cross-validation results should be similar but provide confidence intervals. If the single split was lucky/unlucky, CV gives a more honest estimate.
-->

---

## Walkthrough #8: Hyperparameter Tuning with Grid Search

### Train a Random Forest classifier

> 🌲 *"Let's build on what we've learned with Random Forests and now **optimize** them. We'll use GridSearchCV to find the best hyperparameters."*

```{python}
X = telco_churn[['AvgServiceUsageScore', 'tenure', 'MonthlyCharges']]
y = telco_churn['Churn']

rf = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=2025)
```

### Apply grid search to find optimal hyperparameters

> 🧮 *"Grid Search systematically tries every combination of parameters we define in `param_grid`. We're scoring using **recall** because in churn, catching as many actual churners as possible can be more valuable than just overall accuracy."*

```{python}
param_grid = {
    'n_estimators': [100, 200],
    'max_depth': [4, 8],
    'min_samples_split': [2, 5],
    'min_samples_leaf': [1, 2]
}

grid_search = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=rf,
    param_grid=param_grid,
    cv=5,
    scoring='recall',
    n_jobs=-1,
    verbose=1
)

grid_search.fit(X, y)
```

### Evaluate model improvement

```{python}
best_rf = grid_search.best_estimator_
y_pred = best_rf.predict(X)

print(f"Best Accuracy: {accuracy_score(y, y_pred):.3f}")
print(f"Best Recall: {recall_score(y, y_pred):.3f}")
```

### Interpret the best hyperparameter combination

```{python}
print("Best Parameters Found:", grid_search.best_params_)
```

---

## Exercise #8: Hyperparameter Tuning with Grid Search

### Train a Random Forest classifier

```{python}
X_m = marketing_campaign[['Income', 'TotalSpent', 'TotalChildren']]
y_m = marketing_campaign['Response']

rf_m = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=2025)
```

### Apply grid search to find optimal hyperparameters

```{python}
param_grid_m = {
    'n_estimators': [100, 200],
    'max_depth': [4, 8],
    'min_samples_split': [2, 5],
    'min_samples_leaf': [1, 2]
}

grid_search_m = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=rf_m,
    param_grid=param_grid_m,
    cv=5,
    scoring='recall',
    n_jobs=-1,
    verbose=1
)

grid_search_m.fit(X_m, y_m)
```

### Evaluate model improvement

```{python}
best_rf_m = grid_search_m.best_estimator_
y_m_pred = best_rf_m.predict(X_m)

print(f"Best Accuracy: {accuracy_score(y_m, y_m_pred):.3f}")
print(f"Best Recall: {recall_score(y_m, y_m_pred):.3f}")
```

### Interpret the best hyperparameter combination

```{python}
print("Best Parameters Found:", grid_search_m.best_params_)
```

<!--
### Interpretation Questions

1. Did hyperparameter tuning improve recall compared to the default model in Exercise #4?
2. Look at the best parameters found. Are they at the edges of your grid (suggesting you should expand the search)?
3. Was the computational cost of grid search worth the performance improvement?

> 💡 **Sample Answers:**
>
> 1. Compare your recall here to Exercise #4. Grid search often improves recall by 5-15% through better hyperparameter choices.
>
> 2. If `max_depth=8` (the max in your grid) was selected, you might want to try higher values like 12 or 16. If values are in the middle, the grid was appropriate.
>
> 3. For this small dataset, grid search runs quickly. The improvement in recall is worth it if catching responders has business value. For larger datasets, consider RandomizedSearchCV.
-->

---

# Bonus Challenge: End-to-End ML Pipeline

If you finish early or want additional practice, try this integration challenge using the `marketing_campaign` data:

**Goal:** Build the best model to predict `Response` using everything you've learned.

1. **Feature Engineering:** Create at least one new feature beyond TotalChildren and TotalSpent (e.g., spending per child, years as customer from Dt_Customer)

2. **Model Comparison:** Train both Logistic Regression and Random Forest, use cross-validation to compare them fairly

3. **Optimization:** Use GridSearchCV on your better-performing model

4. **Interpretation:** Write 2-3 sentences explaining which model you'd recommend and why

This is open-ended. There's no single right answer. The goal is to practice the full workflow.

> 💡 **Sample Solution Approach:**
>
> ```python
> # 1. Feature Engineering
> marketing_campaign['SpendingPerChild'] = marketing_campaign['TotalSpent'] / (marketing_campaign['TotalChildren'] + 1)
> marketing_campaign['DaysSinceJoin'] = (pd.Timestamp('2024-01-01') - marketing_campaign['Dt_Customer']).dt.days
>
> # 2. Model Comparison with CV
> features = ['Income', 'TotalSpent', 'TotalChildren', 'SpendingPerChild', 'DaysSinceJoin']
> X = marketing_campaign[features].fillna(0)
> y = marketing_campaign['Response']
>
> X_scaled = StandardScaler().fit_transform(X)
>
> # Compare models
> logreg = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000)
> rf = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=2025)
>
> for name, model in [('Logistic Regression', logreg), ('Random Forest', rf)]:
>     scores = cross_val_score(model, X_scaled, y, cv=5, scoring='recall')
>     print(f"{name}: {scores.mean():.3f} ± {scores.std():.3f}")
>
> # 3. Grid Search on winner
> # 4. Interpretation: "I'd recommend Random Forest because it achieved 
> #    higher recall with consistent performance across folds..."
> ```
